subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 17! In this unit you will explore two beautiful ideas that are hiding everywhere around you: patterns and symmetry. A pattern is anything that follows a rule and repeats or grows in a sensible way. Symmetry is the special balance you see when one part of a shape is a perfect mirror of another part. Once you learn to notice patterns and symmetry, you will start seeing them everywhere – in the tiles of the masjid floor, in the wings of a butterfly, in the beads Halima threads for a necklace, and in the numbers you count every day.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Mathematics 0096 — Stage 3 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: This unit is built for self-paced learning. That means you move at your own speed. Read each explanation slowly, try every worked example yourself on paper, and only move on when it feels clear. Your AI tutor is ready day and night to give you extra patterns to solve, to explain a step in a new way, or to check your answers. Your teacher will also meet with you about twice a week to help with anything tricky. Keep a pencil, some paper and a few colouring pencils beside you as you work – patterns and symmetry are much easier to learn when you draw them.

Why does this matter? Patterns help us predict what comes next. If Yusuf knows the number of dates on the tray grows by five each day, he can work out how many there will be next week without counting one by one. Symmetry helps us understand balance and beauty. Builders use symmetry to make walls that stand straight and strong. Artists use it to make designs that feel calm and pleasing. Even your own body has symmetry – two eyes, two ears, two arms, one on each side. Learning to see these ideas trains your brain to think like a mathematician.

Remember that mathematics is not a subject you can learn only by reading – you must do it. Draw the shapes, colour the halves, continue the sequences out loud, and fold real paper to check your symmetry. If you make a mistake, that is not a failure; it is simply a sign of exactly what to practise next. Every skilled mathematician once sat where you sit now, learning these very same ideas. Let us begin our journey to become pattern and symmetry experts!
Recognise and describe repeating patterns and find the part that repeats (the core).
Recognise and describe growing patterns and say the rule that makes them grow.
Continue and extend patterns by applying the rule correctly.
Create your own patterns using numbers, shapes and colours.
Understand line symmetry (reflective or mirror symmetry) and what a mirror image is.
Find and draw the lines of symmetry in shapes, letters and objects.
Count how many lines of symmetry a shape has (none, one, or many).

concepts.title: What Is a Pattern?
concepts.explanation: A pattern is a sequence or arrangement of things that follows a rule and shows regularity. That means it is not random – there is an idea behind it that lets you know what comes next. Patterns are all around you. Think about the call to prayer that comes five times each day, always in the same order. Think about the days of the week that repeat: Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday, and after Sunday we are back to Monday again. These are patterns because they follow a rule and repeat. Patterns can be made from many…

concepts.title: Repeating Patterns and the Core
concepts.explanation: A repeating pattern is one where a small group of items repeats over and over, like a song chorus you sing again and again. The part that repeats is called the core. Finding the core is the key to understanding any repeating pattern. Look at this pattern of shapes: circle, square, circle, square, circle, square. The core here is circle, square, because that little group of two keeps repeating. Look at this one: red, blue, green, red, blue, green. The core is red, blue, green – a group of three. To find the core,…
concepts.example: Here is a clever trick. Once you know the core, you can work out what comes at any position in the pattern, even far along. Suppose Khadija makes a bracelet with the core red, blue, green (three beads), and she wants to…

concepts.title: Growing Patterns and Their Rules
concepts.explanation: A growing pattern is different from a repeating pattern. Instead of the same group coming back again and again, the numbers change by a clear rule – usually getting bigger, but sometimes getting smaller. To describe a growing pattern, you must find the rule that gets you from one term to the next. Look at the pattern 2, 5, 8, 11, 14. How do you get from one number to the next? From 2 to 5 you add 3. From 5 to 8 you add 3. From 8 to 11 you add 3 again. So the rule is add 3 each time. Once you know the rule, you can…
concepts.example: Some growing patterns get smaller instead of bigger. Look at 25, 20, 15, 10, 5. Here the rule is subtract 5 each time. The numbers shrink by five at every step. A pattern that goes down is still a growing pattern in the…

concepts.title: Special Number Patterns
concepts.explanation: Some number patterns are so useful and so beautiful that they have their own names. You will meet these again and again in mathematics, so it is worth getting to know them now. Counting patterns: adding or subtracting the same amount each time. Example: 5, 10, 15, 20 (add 5).
concepts.example: Doubling patterns: multiplying by 2 each time. Example: 3, 6, 12, 24, 48.

concepts.title: Creating and Extending Patterns
concepts.explanation: Now that you can spot patterns, let us make our own. To create a pattern, follow four simple steps: Choose the elements you want to use – numbers, shapes, colours or objects.
concepts.example: Decide the type – will it be a repeating pattern or a growing pattern?

concepts.title: Understanding Symmetry
concepts.explanation: Now we move to the second big idea of the unit: symmetry. Symmetry happens when one part of something is a mirror image of another part. Something is symmetrical when it can be divided into parts that match exactly. Imagine holding a small mirror up to a shape: if the reflection completes the shape perfectly, the shape has symmetry. Symmetry is found everywhere. In nature, a butterfly's two wings match, and so do the two halves of many leaves and flowers. In buildings, the two sides of a doorway or a bridge often…
concepts.example: The kind of symmetry we study most in this unit is line symmetry, also called reflective symmetry or mirror symmetry. A shape has line symmetry when you can draw a straight line through it so that one half is the exact…

methods.title: How to find the core of a repeating pattern
methods.example: Find the core of this pattern: triangle, square, square, triangle, square, square.
steps: Look for the smallest group of items that seems to repeat.
Write that group out two or three times.
Check it matches the whole pattern. If not, try a bigger group.
The group that works is the core.

methods.title: How to find the rule of a growing pattern
methods.example: Continue for three more terms: square, circle, triangle, square, circle, triangle, ___, ___, ___.
steps: Look at how the numbers change from one term to the next.
Check the how to find the rule of a growing pattern result carefully.
Check the how to find the rule of a growing pattern result carefully.

methods.title: Ask: am I adding, subtracting, or multiplying the same amount?
steps: Check the rule works between two or three pairs.
Apply the rule again and again to continue the pattern.
Check the ask: am i adding, subtracting, or multiplying the same amount? result carefully.

methods.title: How to find lines of symmetry
steps: Try folding the shape down the middle (vertical line).
Try folding it across the middle (horizontal line).
Try folding it corner to corner (diagonal lines).
Count each fold where the halves match exactly.

methods.title: A shrinking pattern
steps: Solution: The numbers go down by 5 each time (25, 20 … subtract 5).
So after 20 comes 20 − 5 = 15, and after 5 comes 5 − 5 = 0.
The full pattern is 25, 20, 15, 10, 5, 0.
Worked Example 6: Which bead colour?

methods.title: Which bead colour?
steps: Solution: The numbers go down by 5 each time (25, 20 … subtract 5).
So after 20 comes 20 − 5 = 15, and after 5 comes 5 − 5 = 0.
The full pattern is 25, 20, 15, 10, 5, 0.
Worked Example 6: Which bead colour?

workedExamples.title: Find the core
workedExamples.prompt: Find the core of this pattern: triangle, square, square, triangle, square, square.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Look for the smallest group that repeats. The group triangle, square, square appears, then appears again. So the core is triangle, square, square – a group of three. Test it: writing the core twice gives triangle, square, square, triangle, square, square, which matches perfectly.

workedExamples.title: Continue a repeating pattern
workedExamples.prompt: Continue for three more terms: square, circle, triangle, square, circle, triangle, ___, ___, ___.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The core is square, circle, triangle. After triangle, the core starts again: square, circle, triangle. So the next three terms are square, circle, triangle.

workedExamples.title: Find the rule and extend
workedExamples.solution: Solution: From 4 to 7 is add 3; from 7 to 10 is add 3; from 10 to 13 is add 3. The rule is add 3 each time. So 13 + 3 = 16, 16 + 3 = 19, 19 + 3 = 22. The next three terms are 16, 19, 22.

workedExamples.title: A doubling pattern
workedExamples.solution: Solution: From 2 to 4 you multiply by 2; from 4 to 8 you multiply by 2; from 8 to 16 you multiply by 2. The rule is multiply by 2 (double) each time. So 16 × 2 = 32, 32 × 2 = 64, 64 × 2 = 128. The next three terms are 32, 64, 128.

workedExamples.title: A shrinking pattern
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The numbers go down by 5 each time (25, 20 … subtract 5). So after 20 comes 20 − 5 = 15, and after 5 comes 5 − 5 = 0. The full pattern is 25, 20, 15, 10, 5, 0. Worked Example 6: Which bead colour? Amina threads beads in the core red, blue, green, over and over. What colour is the 10th bead? Solution: The core has 3 beads, so green sits at every position that is a multiple of 3 (3rd, 6th, 9th …). The 9th bead is green, so the 10th bead starts the core again and is red. (Positions 1, 4, 7, 10 are all red.)

workedExamples.title: Which bead colour?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The numbers go down by 5 each time (25, 20 … subtract 5). So after 20 comes 20 − 5 = 15, and after 5 comes 5 − 5 = 0. The full pattern is 25, 20, 15, 10, 5, 0. Worked Example 6: Which bead colour? Amina threads beads in the core red, blue, green, over and over. What colour is the 10th bead? Solution: The core has 3 beads, so green sits at every position that is a multiple of 3 (3rd, 6th, 9th …). The 9th bead is green, so the 10th bead starts the core again and is red. (Positions 1, 4, 7, 10 are all red.)

workedExamples.title: Count the lines of symmetry
workedExamples.prompt: How many lines of symmetry does a rectangle (not a square) have?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Try each direction. A vertical line down the middle splits it into two matching halves – yes. A horizontal line across the middle also splits it into two matching halves – yes. A diagonal line does not give matching halves for a rectangle. So a rectangle has 2 lines of symmetry.

workedExamples.title: Sort letters by symmetry
workedExamples.prompt: How many lines of symmetry does each letter have: H, S, X?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The letter H has 2 lines (one vertical, one horizontal). The letter X also has 2 lines (one vertical, one horizontal). The letter S has 0 lines – you cannot fold it into matching halves in any direction, although it does look the same when turned upside down (that is a different kind of symmetry). So H = 2, X = 2, S = 0.

workedExamples.title: State how many lines of symmetry each shape has
workedExamples.prompt: State how many lines of symmetry each shape has: a) square b) rectangle (not a square) c) equilateral triangle d) circle.

workedExamples.title: Practice 10
workedExamples.prompt: Sort these letters by their lines of symmetry: A, H, O, F, X. (How many does each have?)

workedExamples.title: Identify the rule for each pattern
workedExamples.prompt: Identify the rule for each pattern: a) 3, 8, 13, 18 … b) 2, 6, 18, 54 … c) 100, 90, 80, 70 …
workedExamples.solution: A = 1, H = 2, O = infinite (many), F = 0, X = 2.

workedExamples.title: Practice 12
workedExamples.prompt: The triangular numbers are 1, 3, 6, 10, 15 … Write the next three terms and explain the rule.
workedExamples.solution: 1) Next terms 21, 28, 36 – the gap grows by one each time (+6, +7, +8).

activities.title: Pattern Hunt Around the House
activities.materials: paper, a pencil, and your sharp eyes.
steps: Walk around your home, the masjid, or outside and look for at least five patterns.
For each pattern, draw it and write whether it is a repeating or a growing pattern.
Write down the core (for repeating patterns) or the rule (for growing patterns).
Make a little ‘Pattern Museum’ by displaying your drawings for your family to see.
Patterns hide in tiles, mats, cloth, fences, brickwork and leaves. How many can you find?

activities.title: Bead Bracelet Patterns
activities.materials: beads, buttons, dried beans or small stones, and string or thread.
steps: Choose a core of two or three colours, for example red, blue, green.
Thread your beads following the core, over and over, to make a bracelet.
Challenge a family member to work out your core and say what colour the next bead should be.
Now make a growing pattern instead: 1 red, then 2 blue, then 3 green, and so on.

activities.title: Fold-and-Cut Symmetry
activities.materials: paper and scissors (ask an adult to help with cutting).
steps: Fold a piece of paper in half. The fold will be your line of symmetry.
Draw half of a shape – a heart, a leaf, or a star – against the fold.
Cut along your drawing while the paper stays folded.
Open the paper. Both halves match! Mark the fold line and see that it is a line of symmetry.
Try different shapes and folds. Can you make a shape with two lines of symmetry by folding twice?

activities.title: Butterfly Paint Blot
activities.materials: paper, a little paint or thick coloured water.
steps: Fold a paper in half and open it flat again.
Drop a few blobs of paint onto one side only, near the fold.
Fold the paper again and press gently so the paint spreads.
Open it up to reveal a symmetrical butterfly design. Trace the line of symmetry down the fold.

activities.title: The Missing Term Game (Family Game)
activities.materials: paper, a pencil, and one or more players.
steps: One player writes a pattern but leaves a gap, such as 4, 8, ___, 16, 20.
The other players work out the rule and fill in the missing term.
Swap roles and try harder patterns, including doubling patterns like 2, 4, ___, 16.
Score a point for each correct answer. Play to ten points.

activities.title: Symmetry Letter Sort (Investigation)
activities.materials: paper, a pencil, and a small mirror if you have one.
steps: Write out the capital letters A, B, C, D, E, H, I, M, O, T, U, V, W, X, Y.
For each letter, use a mirror or fold to test for lines of symmetry.
Sort the letters into groups: no lines, one line, and more than one line.
Which letter has the most lines of symmetry? Compare your answer with a friend.

After your activities, talk or write about: Which activity helped you understand patterns or symmetry best? What was the trickiest part? Where did you find the most surprising pattern or line of symmetry? Share your answers with your teacher at your next session.

rules.title: Repeating Pattern Rule
rules.text: A repeating pattern is built from a core that repeats over and over. The core is the smallest group that repeats. Example core: red, blue, green. To find a far-off term, count in groups the size of the core.

rules.title: Growing Pattern Rule
rules.text: A growing pattern changes by the same rule every step. Look for add, subtract, or multiply by the same amount. Check the rule works for two or three pairs before you continue.

rules.title: Line Symmetry Rule
rules.text: A line of symmetry splits a shape into two matching mirror halves. If you fold along the line, the two halves land exactly on top of each other. Always test vertical, horizontal and diagonal lines.

terms: An arrangement that follows a rule
The repeating part of a repeating pattern
How a growing pattern changes each step
One item or number in a pattern
When one part mirrors another part
The mirror line dividing a shape into equal halves
The flipped picture, as seen in a mirror
Flipping a shape across a line
commonMistakes: Test the core by repeating it and matching
Keep the same rule every step
Letters like F and G have no line of symmetry

realProblems.prompt: At Hargeisa market, Sumaya stacks baskets: 6 baskets on Monday, 9 on Tuesday, 12 on Wednesday. If the pattern continues, how many on Friday?
realProblems.answer: 1) Add 3 each day: Mon 6, Tue 9, Wed 12, Thu 15, Fri 18 → 18 baskets.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 1) Add 3 each day: Mon 6, Tue 9, Wed 12, Thu 15, Fri 18 → 18 baskets.

realProblems.prompt: A theatre has rows of seats. Row 1 has 10 seats, Row 2 has 12, Row 3 has 14. How many seats are in Row 6? Describe the rule.
realProblems.answer: Add 2 each row: 10, 12, 14, 16, 18, 20 → Row 6 has 20 seats; rule is start at 10 and add 2 for each new row.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Add 2 each row: 10, 12, 14, 16, 18, 20 → Row 6 has 20 seats; rule is start at 10 and add 2 for each new row.

realProblems.prompt: Hassan folds paper in half and cuts half of a butterfly along the fold. When he opens it, how many lines of symmetry does the butterfly have, and where is it?
realProblems.answer: One line of symmetry, straight down the fold line where the paper was folded.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: One line of symmetry, straight down the fold line where the paper was folded.

realProblems.prompt: Zahra threads a necklace with the core red, blue, green, blue (4 beads). If it has 20 beads, how many are blue?
realProblems.answer: The core has 4 beads with blue in positions 2 and 4, so blue appears twice in every 4 beads. In 20 beads there are 20 ÷ 4 = 5 cores, so 10 blue beads.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: The core has 4 beads with blue in positions 2 and 4, so blue appears twice in every 4 beads. In 20 beads there are 20 ÷ 4 = 5 cores, so 10 blue beads.

realProblems.prompt: Idris notices the number of dates on the tray each day: 2, 4, 8, 16 … On which day will there first be more than 30 dates?
realProblems.answer: Day 1: 2, Day 2: 4, Day 3: 8, Day 4: 16, Day 5: 32 → first more than 30 on Day 5.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Day 1: 2, Day 2: 4, Day 3: 8, Day 4: 16, Day 5: 32 → first more than 30 on Day 5.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: The triangular numbers are 1, 3, 6, 10, 15 … Write the next three terms and explain the rule.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 1) Next terms 21, 28, 36 – the gap grows by one each time (+6, +7, +8).

reasoningPrompts.prompt: The Fibonacci sequence is 1, 1, 2, 3, 5, 8 … Write the next four terms and explain how each is made.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Next four: 13, 21, 34, 55 – each number is the sum of the two before it (5+8=13, 8+13=21, 13+21=34, 21+34=55).

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Farah makes a bead pattern with the core green, green, yellow (3 beads). What colour is the 15th bead? Explain your thinking.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: The core has 3 beads, so yellow is every 3rd bead (3rd, 6th, 9th, 12th, 15th). The 15th bead is yellow.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Draw a shape with exactly four lines of symmetry, then a shape with exactly one line of symmetry. Mark all the lines.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: A square shows four lines of symmetry (vertical, horizontal, two diagonals); an isosceles triangle or the letter A shows exactly one.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the key idea in Creating and Extending Patterns.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Now that you can spot patterns, let us make our own. To create a pattern, follow four simple steps: Choose the elements you want to use – numbers, shapes, colours or objects.

questions.question: How many edges does a cube have?

selfAssessment: I can this unit is built for self-paced learning. That means you move at your own speed. Read each explanation slowly, try every worked example yourself on paper, and only move on when it feels clear. Your AI tutor is ready day and night to give you extra patterns to solve, to explain a step in a new way, or to check your answers. Your teacher will also meet with you about twice a week to help with anything tricky. Keep a pencil, some paper and a few colouring pencils beside you as you work – patterns and symmetry are much easier to learn when you draw them.

I can why does this matter? Patterns help us predict what comes next. If Yusuf knows the number of dates on the tray grows by five each day, he can work out how many there will be next week without counting one by one. Symmetry helps us understand balance and beauty. Builders use symmetry to make walls that stand straight and strong. Artists use it to make designs that feel calm and pleasing. Even your own body has symmetry – two eyes, two ears, two arms, one on each side. Learning to see these ideas trains your brain to think like a mathematician.

I can remember that mathematics is not a subject you can learn only by reading – you must do it. Draw the shapes, colour the halves, continue the sequences out loud, and fold real paper to check your symmetry. If you make a mistake, that is not a failure; it is simply a sign of exactly what to practise next. Every skilled mathematician once sat where you sit now, learning these very same ideas. Let us begin our journey to become pattern and symmetry experts!
I can recognise and describe repeating patterns and find the part that repeats (the core).
I can recognise and describe growing patterns and say the rule that makes them grow.
I can continue and extend patterns by applying the rule correctly.
I can create your own patterns using numbers, shapes and colours.
I can understand line symmetry (reflective or mirror symmetry) and what a mirror image is.
